rationale.R1.1: The contribution claim ('verbal confidence improves trust calibration') rests on a conceptual conflation: calibration is alignment of trust with accuracy, but the study measures only mean trust magnitude. Reconstructing the thesis requires clarifying or revising what the study actually shows.
rationale.R1.2: Load-bearing: the logical chain is broken at the inference step. Higher mean Likert trust scores do not entail better calibration; the discussion treats them as equivalent. The post-hoc conclusion that 'coarse-grained verbal categories yield more accurate mental models' is not licensed by the reported statistics.
rationale.R1.3: Load-bearing: the central evidence-claim gap is fundamental. No accuracy data are reported, so no calibration metric (e.g., ECE, Brier score decomposition, or even within-subject trust-vs-correctness correlation) can be computed. The claim in the title construct ('calibration') is unsupported by any reported measure.
rationale.R1.6: Writing polish is naturally within scope of 'improve this draft'; several passages (operationalization paragraph, discussion hedging) are also prose-improvable independent of the validity issues.

parsed.clarification_question: Should improvements include substantive validity critique—specifically, that the Likert trust-score operationalization does not measure calibration and that the discussion's conclusions therefore do not follow from the data—or is scope restricted to prose and presentation quality only?
